title: 🎙️ Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today we explore how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture — from festivals and community radio to digital archives and open-source arts tools. We're looking for stories where creativity meets technology in small-town contexts, and where local artists and storytellers are finding new ways to reach audiences beyond geography.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling' — how Cariboo and rural BC communities create, preserve, and share culture.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Most genuine arts/culture/media journalism should land well above 0, even without a Cariboo tie-in:
- 80-100: Cariboo/Williams Lake-specific arts and culture news — community arts council grants or murals, local festivals, Indigenous film/storytelling, community theatre, museum/heritage programs, a Cariboo author or artist receiving an award, community radio or local publishing.
- 55-79: Rural BC or Canadian arts/culture journalism without a specific Cariboo tie — CBC Arts features on Indigenous storytelling, regional festivals, community media projects, heritage preservation, creative-economy stories in small towns, digital storytelling tools and platforms.
- 30-54: General arts, culture, media, or entertainment journalism with real creative/storytelling substance but no rural or community angle (national awards shows, mainstream film/TV/music industry coverage, publishing industry news).
- 15-29: Tangentially creative content (a tech feature that touches on media/publishing/streaming) or local Cariboo civic news with no arts/culture angle.
- 0-14: No arts, culture, media, or storytelling dimension at all.

Strong fits include: community arts journalism (arts councils, local festivals, heritage programs), oral history and cultural preservation projects, Indigenous film and storytelling, community theatre and local media, rural arts funding and the creative economy in small towns, and digital storytelling platforms and tools. Also strong fits: community media tech (podcasting, streaming, local publishing), creative tech economy, open-source arts tools, Cariboo arts festivals using digital outreach, animation/film/media production in rural BC, local radio/podcast infrastructure, and how rural communities use technology for creative expression. A story about a Cariboo community mural, a CBC Arts feature on BC Indigenous storytelling, a rural arts council receiving grants, or a Cariboo author/artist shortlisted for an award is a strong fit even without an explicit technology angle — cultural journalism matters to this audience. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): medical and health research with no cultural/storytelling angle; working lands and resource economy stories with no cultural framing; wildlife and nature stories without creative or community media context; data centre infrastructure and AI investment news; geopolitical conflict, war, and crime coverage; financial market and stock movement stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Congo's Ebola outbreak has killed 600 and is reaching new provinces
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Congo's latest Ebola outbreak has killed 600 people and is now spreading into provinces that had been untouched. Authorities have logged 1,759 confirmed cases since declaring the outbreak on May 15, and new suspected cases have surfaced in Kisangani, in Tshopo province, well beyond the epicenter in Ituri. — Read the rest

The post Congo's Ebola outbreak has killed 600 and is reaching new provinces appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Quesnel Cariboo Observer] Quesnel Safeway opens hidden time capsule after nearly 40 years
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

## Quesnel Safeway opens hidden time capsule after nearly 40 years Published 10:13 am Friday, July 10, 2026 **By Laisa Conde** 1/7 A few of the items that were kept in the time capsule found at Quesnel's Safeway. (Laísa Condé/Quesnel Cariboo Observer) A dusty bucket hidden behind Quesnel’s Safeway roof for nearly four decades opened a remarkable window into life in 1987. Gloria Moskalyk, manager at Safeway, said about three years ago her office was going through some lighting upgrades. By the time the contractor finished their work one morning, they left a time capsule sitting on her desk.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Castro's supercow got a state obituary and a marble statue
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

On a single day in January 1982, a Cuban cow named Ubre Blanca ("White Udder") produced 109.5 liters of milk — "more than four times a typical cow's production," according to Wikipedia. Guinness World Records recognized that mark, along with her 24,268.9-liter output in a single lactation period. — Read the rest

The post Castro's supercow got a state obituary and a marble statue appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

AI risk represents the apex of growth mania, where a few tech elites gamble with humanity’s future. They take these risks without our consent and without our interests in mind. The result could be humanity being replaced as Earth’s most intelligent species by a silicon-based successor.

items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Dutch architectural firm MVRDV has completed a green-roof residential development in Eindhoven, the Netherlands, bringing 237 new homes into the city’s historic Bergen district. Dubbed Nieuw Bergen, the project combines new construction with the repurposing of two existing buildings, adding housing while respecting the area’s established scale.

Continue Reading

items.title: [MacRumors] Tim Cook and John Ternus Attend Sun Valley Conference Together
items.content_html:
🎨 ✏️

Apple CEO Tim Cook and incoming CEO John Ternus are attending this year's Sun Valley Conference, according to Forbes.


Allen & Co., the boutique investment firm behind the event, uses the conference as an annual excuse to put media, tech, and finance leaders in the same rooms for a week of closed-door talks at the Sun Valley Lodge. This year's event began on July 7 and is expected to run through July 11.

This marks Cook's final Sun Valley trip as Apple's chief executive officer. Apple announced in April that Ternus will take the CEO title on September 1, ending Cook's 15-year run atop the company as he moves into the newly created executive chairman role.

Apple's SVP of Services and Health, Eddy Cue, is also attending the Sun Valley Conference again this year. Cue has been a regular at the conference in previous years.

This year's guest list places Apple's leadership alongside a heavy concentration of AI industry figures, including OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, OpenAI chairman Bret Taylor, OpenAI president Greg Brockman, Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai, Palantir CEO Alex Karp, and Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei.

Beyond the AI contingent, this year's attendees include Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Amazon's Jeff Bezos, YouTube CEO Neal Mohan, Netflix co-CEO Ted Sarandos, Warner Bros. Discovery CEO David Zaslav, Home Depot co-founder Ken Langone, and LinkedIn co-founder Reid Hoffman.

Sun Valley's reputation as a dealmaking backdrop goes back decades. Casual conversations at the resort are widely credited with laying the groundwork for Disney buying ABC in 1995, Bezos picking up The Washington Post in 2013, and Verizon's 2014 deal for AOL.
Tags: Eddy Cue, John Ternus, Tim Cook

This article, "Tim Cook and John Ternus Attend Sun Valley Conference Together" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums
